mn43:1.2: At one time the _Bhagavā_ was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn43:1.3: Then, in the evening, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhika, having emerged from seclusion, went to Venerable Sāriputta. Having gone to him, he exchanged greetings with Venerable Sāriputta.
mn43:1.4: After exchanging courteous and memorable talk, he sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhika said this to Venerable Sāriputta:
mn43:10.1: “Friend, by purified mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇena*], detached from the five *indriyas*, what can be cognized?”
mn43:10.2: “Friend, by purified mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇena*], detached from the five *indriyas*, the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*] can be cognized as ‘space is infinite,’ the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*] can be cognized as ‘consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is infinite,’ and the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*] can be cognized as ‘there is nothing.’”
mn43:11.1: “But, friend, by what does one understand a cognizable *dhamma*?”
mn43:11.2: “Friend, one understands a cognizable *dhamma* with the eye of discernment [*paññā*].”
mn43:12.2: “Friend, discernment [*paññā*] is for direct knowing, for full understanding, and for abandoning.”
mn43:13.1: “And, friend, how many conditions are there for the arising of right view?”
mn43:13.2: “Friend, there are two conditions for the arising of right view—
mn43:13.3: the voice of another, and appropriate attention [*yoniso manasikāra*].
mn43:13.4: These, friend, are the two conditions for the arising of right view.”
mn43:14.1: “And, friend, supported by how many factors does right view have the fruit of liberation of mind and the benefit of the fruit of liberation of mind, and have the fruit of liberation by discernment and the benefit of the fruit of liberation by discernment?”
mn43:14.2: “Friend, supported by five factors, right view has the fruit of liberation of mind and the benefit of the fruit of liberation of mind, and has the fruit of liberation by discernment and the benefit of the fruit of liberation by discernment.
mn43:14.3: Here, friend, right view is supported by ethical conduct, supported by learning, supported by discussion, supported by settling [*samatha*], and supported by insight [*vipassanā*].
mn43:14.4: Supported by these five factors, friend, right view has the fruit of liberation of mind and the benefit of the fruit of liberation of mind, and has the fruit of liberation by discernment and the benefit of the fruit of liberation by discernment.”
mn43:16.1: “But, friend, how does renewed existence in the future come to be?”
mn43:16.2: “For beings obstructed by blindness [*avijjā*] and bound by *taṇhā*, delighting now here, now there—
mn43:16.3: in this way renewed existence in the future comes to be.”
mn43:17.1: “But, friend, how does renewed existence in the future not come to be?”
mn43:17.2: “Through the fading away of blindness [*avijjā*], through the arising of knowledge, through the cessation of *taṇhā*—
mn43:17.3: in this way renewed existence in the future does not come to be.”
mn43:18.2: “Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], and has *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*]—
mn43:18.3: this is called the first *jhāna*, friend.”
mn43:19.1: “And, friend, how many factors does the first *jhāna* have?”
mn43:19.3: Here, for a *bhikkhu* who has attained the first *jhāna*, thought [*vitakka*] is present, examination [*vicāra*] is present, *pīti* is present, *sukha* is present, and singleness of mind [*cittekaggatā*] is present.
mn43:19.4: In this way, friend, the first *jhāna* has five factors.”
mn43:2.2: To what extent, friend, is one called lacking discernment?”
mn43:2.3: “‘He does not understand, he does not understand,’ friend — that is why one is called lacking discernment.
mn43:2.4: And what does he not understand?
mn43:2.5: He does not understand, ‘This is *dukkha*’; he does not understand, ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; he does not understand, ‘This is the ending of *dukkha*’; he does not understand, ‘This is the practice leading to the ending of *dukkha*.’
mn43:2.6: ‘He does not understand, he does not understand,’ friend — that is why one is called lacking discernment.”
mn43:2.7: “Sādhu, friend,” Venerable Mahākoṭṭhika said, approving and agreeing with what Venerable Sāriputta had said. Then he asked Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn43:20.1: “And, friend, with how many factors abandoned and with how many factors possessed is the first *jhāna*?”
mn43:20.2: “Friend, the first *jhāna* has five factors abandoned and is possessed of five factors.
mn43:20.3: Here, for a *bhikkhu* who has attained the first *jhāna*, sensual desire is abandoned, ill will is abandoned, dullness and drowsiness are abandoned, restlessness and remorse are abandoned, and doubt is abandoned;
mn43:20.4: thought [*vitakka*] is present, examination [*vicāra*] is present, *pīti* is present, *sukha* is present, and singleness of mind [*cittekaggatā*] is present.
mn43:20.5: In this way, friend, the first *jhāna* has five factors abandoned and is possessed of five factors.”
mn43:21.1: “These five *indriyas*, friend, have different fields and different ranges, and they do not experience each other’s fields and ranges, that is:—
mn43:21.2: the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], the ear *indriya* [*sotindriyaṁ*], the nose *indriya* [*ghānindriyaṁ*], the tongue *indriya* [*jivhindriyaṁ*], and the body *indriya* [*kāyindriyaṁ*].
mn43:21.3: For these five *indriyas*, friend, which have different fields and different ranges and do not experience each other’s fields and ranges, what is their support, and who experiences their fields and ranges?”
mn43:21.4: “These five *indriyas*, friend, have different fields and different ranges, and they do not experience each other’s fields and ranges, that is:—
mn43:21.5: the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], the ear *indriya* [*sotindriyaṁ*], the nose *indriya* [*ghānindriyaṁ*], the tongue *indriya* [*jivhindriyaṁ*], and the body *indriya* [*kāyindriyaṁ*].
mn43:21.6: For these five *indriyas*, friend, which have different fields and different ranges and do not experience each other’s fields and ranges, mind is their support, and mind experiences their fields and ranges.”
mn43:22.11: “Just now, friend, we understand Venerable Sāriputta’s statement in this way:
mn43:22.13: But just now we also understand Venerable Sāriputta’s statement in this way:
mn43:22.15: How, then, friend, should the meaning of this statement be seen?”
mn43:22.16: “Then, friend, I will make a simile for you;
mn43:22.17: for by means of a simile some wise people here understand the meaning of what is said.
mn43:22.18: Just as, friend, for an oil lamp that is burning, depending on the flame the light is seen, and depending on the light the flame is seen;
mn43:22.19: just so, friend, life stands depending on heat, and heat stands depending on life.”
mn43:22.2: the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], the ear *indriya* [*sotindriyaṁ*], the nose *indriya* [*ghānindriyaṁ*], the tongue *indriya* [*jivhindriyaṁ*], and the body *indriya* [*kāyindriyaṁ*].
mn43:22.3: Depending on what, friend, do these five *indriyas* stand?”
mn43:22.5: the eye *indriya* [*cakkhundriyaṁ*], the ear *indriya* [*sotindriyaṁ*], the nose *indriya* [*ghānindriyaṁ*], the tongue *indriya* [*jivhindriyaṁ*], and the body *indriya* [*kāyindriyaṁ*].
mn43:22.6: These five *indriyas*, friend, stand depending on life.”
mn43:22.7: “And life, friend, depending on what does it stand?”
mn43:22.9: “And heat, friend, depending on what does it stand?”
mn43:23.1: “Then, friend, are those very same life-*saṅkhāras* the things to be felt, or are life-*saṅkhāras* one thing and the things to be felt another?”
mn43:23.2: “Friend, those life-*saṅkhāras* are not the very same things as the things to be felt.
mn43:23.3: For if those life-*saṅkhāras* were those things to be felt, then for a *bhikkhu* who has attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*], emergence would not be discerned.
mn43:23.4: But because life-*saṅkhāras* are one thing and the things to be felt another, therefore for a *bhikkhu* who has attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*], emergence is discerned.”
mn43:24.1: “When, friend, how many *dhammas* abandon this body, so that this body lies discarded, cast aside, like senseless wood?”
mn43:24.2: “When, friend, three *dhammas* abandon this body — life, heat, and consciousness [*viññāṇa*] — then this body lies discarded, cast aside, like senseless wood.”
mn43:25.1: “This person, friend, who is dead, who has passed away, and this *bhikkhu* who has attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*] — what is the distinction between them?”
mn43:25.2: “For this person, friend, who is dead, who has passed away, bodily *saṅkhāras* [*kāyasaṅkhārā*] have ceased and stilled, verbal *saṅkhāras* [*vacīsaṅkhārā*] have ceased and stilled, mental *saṅkhāras* [*cittasaṅkhārā*] have ceased and stilled, life is exhausted, heat has subsided, and the *indriyas* are broken up.
mn43:25.3: And for this *bhikkhu*, friend, who has attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*], bodily *saṅkhāras* [*kāyasaṅkhārā*] have ceased and stilled, verbal *saṅkhāras* [*vacīsaṅkhārā*] have ceased and stilled, mental *saṅkhāras* [*cittasaṅkhārā*] have ceased and stilled, but life is not exhausted, heat has not subsided, and the *indriyas* are clear.
mn43:25.4: This person, friend, who is dead, who has passed away, and this *bhikkhu* who has attained the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodhaṁ*] — this is their distinction.”
mn43:26.1: “And, friend, how many conditions are there for the attainment of the liberation of mind that is neither *dukkha* nor *sukha*?”
mn43:26.2: “Friend, there are four conditions for the attainment of the liberation of mind that is neither *dukkha* nor *sukha*.
mn43:26.3: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, with the giving up of *sukha* and the giving up of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of gladness and sadness, enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither *dukkha* nor *sukha* and has purification of *sati* by equanimity [*upekkhā*].
mn43:26.4: These, friend, are the four conditions for the attainment of the liberation of mind that is neither *dukkha* nor *sukha*.”
mn43:27.1: “And, friend, how many conditions are there for the attainment of the liberation of mind without *nimitta* [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]?”
mn43:27.2: “Friend, there are two conditions for the attainment of the liberation of mind without *nimitta* [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]—
mn43:27.3: non-attention to all *nimittas*, and attention to the element without *nimitta* [*animittāya dhātuyā*].
mn43:27.4: These, friend, are the two conditions for the attainment of the liberation of mind without *nimitta* [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*].”
mn43:27.5: “And, friend, how many conditions are there for the stability of the liberation of mind without *nimitta* [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]?”
mn43:28.1: “Friend, there are three conditions for the stability of the liberation of mind without *nimitta* [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]—
mn43:28.2: non-attention to all *nimittas*, attention to the element without *nimitta* [*animittāya dhātuyā*], and prior preparation.
mn43:28.3: These, friend, are the three conditions for the stability of the liberation of mind without *nimitta* [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*].”
mn43:29.1: “And, friend, how many conditions are there for emergence from the liberation of mind without *nimitta* [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]?”
mn43:29.2: “Friend, there are two conditions for emergence from the liberation of mind without *nimitta* [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]—
mn43:29.3: attention to all *nimittas*, and non-attention to the element without *nimitta* [*animittāya dhātuyā*].
mn43:29.4: These, friend, are the two conditions for emergence from the liberation of mind without *nimitta* [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*].”
mn43:29.5: “And this measureless liberation of mind, friend, and the liberation of mind of nothingness, and the liberation of mind through emptiness, and the liberation of mind without *nimitta* — are these *dhammas* different in meaning and different in wording, or are they one in meaning and only different in wording?”
mn43:3.1: “‘Possessed of discernment,’ ‘possessed of discernment,’ friend, they say.
mn43:3.2: To what extent, friend, is one called possessed of discernment?”
mn43:3.3: “‘He understands, he understands,’ friend — that is why one is called possessed of discernment.
mn43:3.5: He understands, ‘This is *dukkha*’; he understands, ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; he understands, ‘This is the ending of *dukkha*’; he understands, ‘This is the practice leading to the ending of *dukkha*.’
mn43:3.6: ‘He understands, he understands,’ friend — that is why one is called possessed of discernment.”
mn43:30.1: “And this measureless liberation of mind, friend, and the liberation of mind of nothingness, and the liberation of mind through emptiness, and the liberation of mind without *nimitta* — there is a way, friend, according to which these *dhammas* are different in meaning and different in wording;
mn43:30.2: and there is a way, friend, according to which these *dhammas* are one in meaning, differing only in wording.
mn43:31.1: And what, friend, is the way according to which these *dhammas* are different in meaning and different in wording?
mn43:31.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu* dwells pervading one direction with a mind accompanied by goodwill [*mettā*], likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. So above, below, across, everywhere, and in every respect, he dwells pervading the whole world with a mind accompanied by goodwill [*mettā*], abundant, expansive, measureless, free from hostility, free from ill will.
mn43:31.3: With a mind accompanied by compassion [*karuṇā*] …
mn43:31.4: with a mind accompanied by gladness [*muditā*] …
mn43:31.5: with a mind accompanied by equanimity [*upekkhā*], he dwells pervading one direction, likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. So above, below, across, everywhere, and in every respect, he dwells pervading the whole world with a mind accompanied by equanimity [*upekkhā*], abundant, expansive, measureless, free from hostility, free from ill will.
mn43:31.6: This is called the measureless liberation of mind, friend.
mn43:32.1: And what, friend, is the liberation of mind of nothingness?
mn43:32.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, completely transcending the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘there is nothing.’
mn43:32.3: This is called the liberation of mind of nothingness, friend.
mn43:33.1: And what, friend, is the liberation of mind through emptiness?
mn43:33.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the forest, or gone to the root of a tree, or gone to an empty dwelling, reflects thus:
mn43:33.3: ‘This is empty of self or of what belongs to self.’
mn43:33.4: This is called the liberation of mind through emptiness, friend.
mn43:34.1: And what, friend, is the liberation of mind without *nimitta*?
mn43:34.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, through non-attention to all *nimittas*, enters and dwells in mind-*samādhi* without *nimitta* [*animittaṁ cetosamādhiṁ*].
mn43:34.3: This is called the liberation of mind without *nimitta*, friend.
mn43:34.4: This, friend, is the way according to which these *dhammas* are different in meaning and different in wording.
mn43:35.1: And what, friend, is the way according to which these *dhammas* are one in meaning, differing only in wording?
mn43:35.2: Friend, lust makes measure, hate makes measure, delusion makes measure.
mn43:35.3: For a *bhikkhu* with destroyed *āsavas*, these are abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, brought to non-existence, not liable to arise in the future.
mn43:35.4: As many as there are measureless liberations of mind, the unshakeable liberation of mind is declared the highest of them.
mn43:35.5: And that unshakeable liberation of mind is empty of lust, empty of hate, empty of delusion.
mn43:36.1: Friend, lust is somethingness, hate is somethingness, delusion is somethingness.
mn43:36.2: For a *bhikkhu* with destroyed *āsavas*, these are abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, brought to non-existence, not liable to arise in the future.
mn43:36.3: As many as there are liberations of mind of nothingness, the unshakeable liberation of mind is declared the highest of them.
mn43:36.4: And that unshakeable liberation of mind is empty of lust, empty of hate, empty of delusion.
mn43:37.1: Friend, lust creates *nimitta*, hate creates *nimitta*, delusion creates *nimitta*.
mn43:37.2: For a *bhikkhu* with destroyed *āsavas*, these are abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, brought to non-existence, not liable to arise in the future.
mn43:37.3: As many as there are liberations of mind without *nimitta*, the unshakeable liberation of mind is declared the highest of them.
mn43:37.4: And that unshakeable liberation of mind is empty of lust, empty of hate, empty of delusion.
mn43:37.5: This, friend, is the way according to which these *dhammas* are one in meaning, differing only in wording.”
mn43:37.7: Satisfied, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhika rejoiced in what Venerable Sāriputta had said.
mn43:37.8: The Greater Questions and Answers is finished, the third.
mn43:4.2: To what extent, friend, is it called consciousness [*viññāṇa*]?”
mn43:4.3: “‘It cognizes, it cognizes,’ friend — that is why it is called consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn43:4.5: It cognizes *sukha*, it cognizes *dukkha*, it cognizes what is neither *dukkha* nor *sukha*.
mn43:4.6: ‘It cognizes, it cognizes,’ friend — that is why it is called consciousness [*viññāṇa*].”
mn43:5.3: And is it possible, having separated these *dhammas* out one by one, to describe their distinction?”
mn43:5.6: And it is not possible, having separated these *dhammas* out one by one, to describe their distinction.
mn43:5.7: For what one understands, that one cognizes; and what one cognizes, that one understands.
mn43:5.8: Therefore these *dhammas* are conjoined, not disjoined.
mn43:5.9: And it is not possible, having separated these *dhammas* out one by one, to describe their distinction.”
mn43:6.2: for these *dhammas*, conjoined and not disjoined, what is their distinction?”
mn43:6.4: of these *dhammas*, conjoined and not disjoined, discernment [*paññā*] should be developed, and consciousness [*viññāṇa*] should be fully understood.
mn43:7.2: To what extent, friend, is it called *vedanā*?”
mn43:7.3: “‘One feels, one feels,’ friend — that is why it is called *vedanā*.
mn43:7.5: One feels *sukha*, one feels *dukkha*, one feels what is neither *dukkha* nor *sukha*.
mn43:7.6: ‘One feels, one feels,’ friend — that is why it is called *vedanā*.”
mn43:8.2: To what extent, friend, is it called *saññā*?”
mn43:8.3: “‘One perceives, one perceives,’ friend — that is why it is called *saññā*.
mn43:8.5: One perceives blue, one perceives yellow, one perceives red, one perceives white.
mn43:8.6: ‘One perceives, one perceives,’ friend — that is why it is called *saññā*.”
mn43:9.3: And is it possible, having separated these *dhammas* out one by one, to describe their distinction?”
mn43:9.6: And it is not possible, having separated these *dhammas* out one by one, to describe their distinction.
mn43:9.7: For what one feels, that one perceives; what one perceives, that one cognizes.
mn43:9.8: Therefore these *dhammas* are conjoined, not disjoined.
mn43:9.9: And it is not possible, having separated these *dhammas* out one by one, to describe their distinction.”
